did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used AI/ML methods for the automatic diagnosis of melanoma. Specifically, it employed a hybrid descriptor combining the ABCD rule, texture features (GLCM, GLRLM, HOG, LBP, Tamura, Box-Counting), and transfer learning features from pre-trained Convolutional Neural Networks (AlexNet, CaffeNet, Vgg-f). These features were classified using a MultiLayer Perceptron (MLP) classifier, with comparisons to SVM and Random Forest, to distinguish between melanoma and non-melanoma skin lesions using dermoscopic images from the PH2 and DermIS databases.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the design and evaluation of a computational method for melanoma diagnosis. It details specific materials and methods (ABCD rule, texture features, transfer learning), describes experiments conducted on public databases (PH2 and DermIS), and reports new results (accuracy of 92.1%, Kappa index of 0.8346) using classifiers like MLP, SVM, and Random Forest. It is published in the 2018 IEEE Symposium on Computers and Communications (ISCC).

title: Combining ABCD Rule, Texture Features and Transfer Learning in Automatic Diagnosis of Melanoma
journal: 2018 IEEE Symposium on Computers and Communications (ISCC)
brief_summary: This study developed a computational method to assist in the automatic diagnosis of melanoma versus non-melanoma skin lesions using dermoscopic images. The proposed approach utilized a hybrid descriptor combining features from the ABCD rule, various texture descriptors (GLCM, GLRLM, HOG, LBP, Tamura, Box-Counting), and transfer learning features from pre-trained Convolutional Neural Networks (AlexNet, CaffeNet, VGG-f). These features were classified using a MultiLayer Perceptron (MLP), with comparisons made against Support Vector Machine (SVM) and Random Forest classifiers. The evaluation was conducted on 406 images from the combined PH2 and DermIS public databases. The method achieved a classification accuracy of 92.1% and a Kappa index of 0.8346, demonstrating promising results for melanoma detection.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target class for diagnosis. The study aims to distinguish melanoma from non-melanoma lesions. In the PH2 database, there were 40 melanomas. In the DermIS database, there were 119 melanomas.

The negative class in the binary classification task. In the PH2 database, this included 80 common nevi and 80 atypical nevi. In the DermIS database, this included 87 non-melanomas. The study groups these as 'non-melanoma' for the final classification task.
A specific type of benign lesion present in the PH2 database (80 images), grouped into the 'non-melanoma' category for the main experiment.
A specific type of benign lesion present in the PH2 database (80 images), grouped into the 'non-melanoma' category for the main experiment.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin lesions into 'melanoma' or 'non-melanoma' categories.
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the goal is to 'classify skin lesions' and 'distinguish between melanoma and non-melanoma skin lesions'. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (melanoma vs. non-melanoma) using a MultiLayer Perceptron (MLP), SVM, and Random Forest classifiers. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, and Kappa index on dermoscopic images from the PH2 and DermIS databases.

datasets_used.name: PH2 Dataset
datasets_used.description: A public dermoscopic image database for research and benchmarking, containing images diagnosed by a specialist with binary masks of segmentation. Images are in RGB color system with a resolution of 768x560 pixels.

use.training: Used for classification experiments (combined with DermIS for the main hybrid descriptor evaluation).
use.validation: Used for classification experiments (combined with DermIS for the main hybrid descriptor evaluation).
use.testing: Used for classification experiments (combined with DermIS for the main hybrid descriptor evaluation).

datasets_used.name: DermIS
datasets_used.description: The Dermatology Information System database, a public collection of dermoscopic images diagnosed by a specialist with binary masks of segmentation.

use.training: Used for classification experiments (combined with PH2 for the main hybrid descriptor evaluation).
use.validation: Used for classification experiments (combined with PH2 for the main hybrid descriptor evaluation).
use.testing: Used for classification experiments (combined with PH2 for the main hybrid descriptor evaluation).

datasets_used.name: PH2 + DermIS
datasets_used.description: The combined dataset formed by union of the PH2 and DermIS databases, used to provide a more significant set of diversified images for the primary evaluation of the proposed method.

use.training: Used as the primary dataset for evaluating the proposed hybrid descriptor method with MLP, SVM, and Random Forest classifiers.
use.validation: Used as the primary dataset for evaluating the proposed hybrid descriptor method with MLP, SVM, and Random Forest classifiers.
use.testing: Used as the primary dataset for evaluating the proposed hybrid descriptor method with MLP, SVM, and Random Forest classifiers.
use.other: Used for robustness testing against segmentation errors (under-segmentation and over-segmentation).

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To compute classification performance metrics including Precision, Recall, Accuracy, and Kappa index by counting True Positives, False Negatives, False Positives, and True Negatives.

To measure the agreement between the classifier's predictions and the ground truth, accounting for chance agreement, and to qualify the accuracy level (Poor, Moderate, Good, Very Good, Excellent) based on Landis and Koch's scale.
To identify the most relevant features by constructing a ranking of attributes from most to least relevant, and testing subsets of the top 5%, 10%, 20%, 30%, 40%, and 50% of attributes.
To compare the performance of the proposed hybrid descriptor method against individual descriptors and other classifiers (SVM, Random Forest) across different databases (PH2, DermIS, PH2+DermIS).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values or significance tests). Performance was evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, and the Kappa index, with comparisons made based on these descriptive metrics rather than statistical significance testing.

PH2.description: 200 images (80 common nevi, 80 atypical nevi, 40 melanomas)
PH2.notes: The proposed method with MLP achieved the best result, considered 'Excellent' according to Landis and Koch. The hybrid descriptor reached the highest rates of accuracy in the PH2 database.

DermIS.notes: The proposed method with MLP reached an accuracy of 89.8% and a Kappa index of 0.792, considered 'Very Good'. The success rate lowered compared to PH2 due to higher similarity among categories in DermIS images.

PH2 + DermIS.notes: The proposed method with MLP achieved an accuracy of 92.1% and a Kappa index of 0.835, considered 'Excellent'. The hybrid descriptor reached the highest rates of accuracy in the three image bases with the MLP classifier.

PH2 + DermIS (Segmentation Errors).description: 406 images with simulated under-segmentation (-10% area) and over-segmentation (+10% area)
PH2 + DermIS (Segmentation Errors).notes: Tests were performed to analyze robustness against segmentation errors. The method obtained a 'Very Good' Kappa index even with errors. Under-segmentation achieved lower success rates (80.0%) than over-segmentation (83.7%).

architecture_used.main_model: Hybrid descriptor classifier using MultiLayer Perceptron (MLP), Support Vector Machine (SVM), and Random Forest. The feature extraction pipeline utilizes pre-trained Convolutional Neural Networks (AlexNet, CaffeNet, Vgg-f) for transfer learning, combined with hand-crafted texture features (GLCM, GLRLM, HOG, LBP, Tamura, Box-Counting) and ABCD rule features.
architecture_used.pretraining: The CNNs (AlexNet, CaffeNet, Vgg-f) were pre-trained on the ImageNet natural image database. Transfer learning was performed by using the penultimate layer of the network as an array of characteristics (4096 attributes per instance).
architecture_used.input_size: Images from PH2 database: 768x560 pixels. Images from DermIS database: Resolution not explicitly specified in text, but used in same pipeline. Pre-processing involved segmentation via binary masks and conversion to HSV color model, extracting features from the sum of S and V bands.

CNN_architectures.name: AlexNet
CNN_architectures.layers: 8 layers (5 convolutional layers followed by 3 fully connected layers, including maximum pooling layers)

CNN_architectures.name: CaffeNet
CNN_architectures.layers: 8 layers (5 convolutional layers, each followed by a pooling layer, and 3 fully connected layers)

CNN_architectures.name: Vgg-f
CNN_architectures.layers: 8 learning layers (5 convolutions and 3 fully connected)

architecture_details.feature_selection: Gain Ratio Information algorithm (Ranker method) used to select top 5% of most relevant attributes.

training_details.classifier: MultiLayer Perceptron (MLP) was the primary classifier used for the final hybrid descriptor. SVM and Random Forest were also tested for comparison.
training_details.attribute_selection_method: Gain Ratio Information algorithm with Ranker
training_details.feature_subset_size: 5% of the most significant attributes (selected from total vector of 12,705 characteristics)

preprocessing_techniques: Segmentation of the region of interest using binary masks provided by the PH2 and DermIS databases
Conversion of images from RGB color model to HSV color model
Extraction of features from the component generated by the sum of the S (Saturation) and V (Value) bands
Simulation of segmentation errors for robustness testing: under-segmentation (decreasing ground-truth area by 10%) and over-segmentation (increasing ground-truth area by 10%)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Under-segmentation was simulated by decreasing the ground-truth area by 10%. Over-segmentation was simulated by increasing the ground-truth area by 10%. These were used to test robustness, not as standard training augmentation.

conclusions: The proposed hybrid descriptor method, combining ABCD rule features, texture features (GLCM, GLRLM, HOG, LBP, Tamura, Box-Counting), and transfer learning features from pre-trained CNNs (AlexNet, CaffeNet, VGG-f), achieved an accuracy of 92.1% and a Kappa index of 0.835 (classified as 'Excellent') on the combined PH2 and DermIS database.

The MultiLayer Perceptron (MLP) classifier outperformed Support Vector Machine (SVM) and Random Forest classifiers when used with the hybrid descriptor across all tested databases (PH2, DermIS, and PH2+DermIS).

Feature selection using the Gain Ratio Information algorithm identified that only 5% of the most relevant attributes were sufficient for optimal performance, with CNN-derived features (VGG-f, AlexNet, CaffeNet) being among the most commonly selected.

The method demonstrated robustness against segmentation errors; even with simulated under-segmentation (-10% area) and over-segmentation (+10% area), the method maintained a 'Very Good' Kappa index (0.5827 and 0.661, respectively), though performance decreased compared to perfect segmentation.

Under-segmentation resulted in lower success rates (80.0% accuracy) compared to over-segmentation (83.7% accuracy), indicating that information from regions close to the lesion edge is essential for accurate classification.

The hybrid descriptor approach achieved higher accuracy rates than individual descriptors and other state-of-the-art methods evaluated in the study, demonstrating the benefit of combining multiple feature types for differentiating melanoma from non-melanoma lesions.

The DermIS database presented higher difficulty in differentiating classes compared to PH2 due to greater similarity among lesion categories, resulting in lower performance metrics (89.8% accuracy) on DermIS alone compared to PH2 (96.5% accuracy).
important_remarks: The study relies on a small total dataset of 406 dermoscopic images (combined PH2 and DermIS), which limits the statistical power and generalizability of the results.

The proposed method was evaluated on only two public databases (PH2 and DermIS); lack of external validation on independent datasets from different institutions or populations is a significant limitation.
Performance varied significantly between databases: accuracy dropped from 96.5% on PH2 to 89.8% on DermIS, indicating potential dataset bias or heterogeneity in lesion characteristics across sources.

The DermIS database presented higher difficulty in differentiating classes due to greater similarity among lesion categories compared to PH2, suggesting the model may struggle with more ambiguous or diverse real-world data.
The paper did not report formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) to validate the significance of performance differences between the proposed method and state-of-the-art comparisons.
Robustness was tested only via simulated segmentation errors (±10% area change); real-world variability in segmentation algorithms or manual annotation errors was not assessed.

The feature selection process retained only 5% of the most relevant attributes (from 12,705 total), which may discard potentially useful information and relies heavily on the specific ranking algorithm used.

Transfer learning features were extracted from CNNs pre-trained on ImageNet (natural images), which may not be optimally suited for medical dermoscopic images, potentially introducing domain shift issues.

The SVM classifier showed significantly lower performance (66.5% accuracy) compared to MLP (92.1%) on the combined dataset, suggesting the choice of classifier is critical and results are not robust across different model types.
Some state-of-the-art comparisons in Table VI used different evaluation metrics or smaller datasets (e.g., Bhati and Singhal used 30 images), making direct performance comparisons difficult.
